dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for a fall with a reported red mark on the neck of a 96-year-old female.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
The patient had a fall and now has a red mark on the back of her neck.
Is she showing any pain or discomfort after the fall?
No pain, shortness of breath, chest pain, or dizziness reported. She's alert to self only, which is believed to be her baseline.
Her initial Glasgow Coma Scale score is 13-14. She is AAOx1; alert to self only, which is consistent with her baseline from medical records.
Her initial blood pressure was 168/90 with a pulse of 97 and respiratory rate of 20.
Oxygen saturation is 96% on room air and blood glucose level of 202 mg/dL.
A 4-lead ECG was placed and shows sinus rhythm.
A 12-lead ECG showed nonspecific ST and T wave abnormalities. Her medical history includes atrial fibrillation.
During transport, a second 12-lead falsely showed signs of an acute MI; likely due to artifact from movement. Manual interpretation found no STEMI or acute infarction.
A third 12-lead was obtained, still showing sinus rhythm without significant changes. No STE or STD observed.
She's been moved to the stretcher with precautions and secured with restraints. Transport is imminent.
Her respiratory rate is now 16, non-labored, with no accessory muscle use.
En route vitals were updated at 177/49 with a pulse of 82 and respiratory rate of 16, with oxygen saturation of 100%.
Patient was reportedly found around 1630 at the care facility on the floor. Her staff suspects she slid from a wheelchair.
Patient is not in pain and shows no neurological changes. Severe confusion limits communication, per facility staff.
Skin appears warm, dry, and of normal color. No signs of trauma other than the red mark described.
No physical pain was reported. No pain with inspiration or expiration was detected during breathing.
Chest assessments revealed pain with deep breathing but no significant distress. Motor function consistent with AAOx1.
FAST exam could not be completed due to severe confusion, which the staff indicates is her baseline.
Medical history includes hyperlipidemia and a prior episode of rhabdomyolysis.
Arrived at the ED and transferred the patient to the bed using the pull sheet method for comfort.
We have informed the ED nurse about her baseline confusion, fall status, and red mark on the neck. No rash or cyanosis observed.
Patient was managed under protocol 4, general trauma management, with a focus on fall history and baseline mental status.
12-lead EKG demonstrated artifact during movement but no STEMI detected. One reading was suspicious for STEMI, but not confirmed.
She is ready for ED admitting procedures with full documentation of her fall and red mark injury.
EMS will remain on standby for any ED follow-up with our team.
Documentation is complete and included in her medical file.
The ED nurse has been briefed to ensure continuity of care regarding the fall and potential cardiac artifact.
